The Story AI summary

Bootstrapped HerdrPlus, a Windows-first fork of the herdr terminal multiplexer, from a folder of reference excerpts into a working repo. Cloned upstream, solved the Zig cross-drive cache build blocker, renamed the binary to herdrplus.exe, and shipped two features end-to-end through a full brainstorm-spec-plan-implement-review-PR pipeline: pane balance (equal-area layout reset, PR #1) and a pane-to-pane message bus (server-side inboxes, label addressing, unread badge, PR #2, protocol 18). The msg bus was implemented by three CLI dev agents (Grok/Antigravity) running inside herdrplus panes, then hardened across two external GPT-5.6 Sol review rounds that caught silent-message-loss bugs the tests missed. Both features were live-verified against the real binary and merged to main.

The Facts deterministic

Decisions

  • Fix the Zig build by pinning ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR to the K: drive (cross-drive relativize panic)
  • Vendor upstream herdr source into the repo (remove nested .git) rather than submodule it
  • Pane balance = equal ideal area, tree-preserving (not full grid re-tile)
  • Route design/PR reviews through Sol56 = GPT-5.6 Sol high via Codex
  • Rename the build binary to herdrplus.exe via [[bin]], keep package name herdr for upstream merges
  • Msg delivery = server inbox + TUI unread badge; NO PTY nudge (unsafe raw-keystroke injection)
  • Pane labels ARE the message addresses, with scoped resolution that refuses ambiguity
  • Build the msg feature with 3 CLI dev agents (Grok/Antigravity) inside herdrplus panes
  • Reopen all agents in YOLO/auto-approve mode; monitor via git ground-truth not screen-scraping
  • Next feature: team spawn primitive (one command to stand up a labeled agent team)

Timeline

Build the Windows preview Repo scaffolding & docs Vendor source & e2e pane tests Pane balance: design, review, build Pane balance: PR, merge, live demos Rename binary to herdrplus.exe Msg bus: brainstorm & Sol56 spec review Msg bus: 3-agent orchestrated build Truecolor investigation & label demo Msg bus: reviews, fixes, merge Memories, checkpoint & export

8	use portable_pty::{native_pty_system, Child, CommandBuilder, MasterPty, PtySize};
9	
10	#[cfg(windows)]
11	pub(crate) struct SpawnedPty {
12	    pub master: Box<dyn MasterPty + Send>,
13	    pub child: Box<dyn Child + Send + Sync>,
14	}
15	
16	#[cfg(windows)]
17	pub(crate) fn spawn_with_portable_pty(
18	    rows: u16,
19	    cols: u16,
20	    cmd: CommandBuilder,
21	) -> std::io::Result<SpawnedPty> {
22	    let pty_system = native_pty_system();
23	    let pair = pty_system
24	        .openpty(PtySize {
25	            rows,
26	            cols,
27	            pixel_width: 0,
28	            pixel_height: 0,
29	        })
30	        .map_err(|err| std::io::Error::other(err.to_string()))?;
31	    let child = pair
32	        .slave
33	        .spawn_command(cmd)
34	        .map_err(|err| std::io::Error::other(err.to_string()))?;
35	
36	    Ok(SpawnedPty {
37	        master: pair.master,
38	        child,
39	    })
40	}
41

1	#[cfg(unix)]
2	mod unix;
3	
4	#[cfg(unix)]
5	pub(crate) use unix::*;
6	
7	#[cfg(windows)]
8	mod windows {
9	    use std::io::{Read, Write};
10	    use std::sync::{mpsc as std_mpsc, Arc, Mutex};
11	    use std::time::Duration;
12	
13	    use bytes::Bytes;
14	    use portable_pty::{MasterPty, PtySize};
15	    use tokio::sync::mpsc;
16	    use tracing::{debug, warn};
17	
18	    pub(crate) struct PtyReadResult {
19	        pub terminal_responses: Vec<Bytes>,
20	    }
21	
22	    type ReadCallback = Box<dyn FnMut(&[u8]) -> PtyReadResult + Send + 'static>;
23	    type ReaderExitCallback = Box<dyn FnOnce() + Send + 'static>;
24	
25	    #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
26	    struct PtyResize {
27	        rows: u16,
28	        cols: u16,
29	        cell_width_px: u32,
30	        cell_height_px: u32,
31	    }
32	
33	    struct PtyResizeRequest {
34	        resize: PtyResize,
35	        terminal_responses: Vec<Bytes>,
36	    }
37	
38	    pub(crate) struct PtyIoActorConfig {
39	        pub pane_id: u32,
40	        pub master: Box<dyn MasterPty + Send>,
41	        pub initially_quiesced: bool,
42	        pub on_read: ReadCallback,
43	        pub on_reader_exit: Option<ReaderExitCallback>,
44	    }
45	
46	    enum PtyIoControlCommand {
47	        Resize(PtyResizeRequest),
48	        Shutdown,
49	    }
50	
51	    #[derive(Clone)]
52	    pub(crate) struct PtyIoActorHandle {
53	        data_tx: mpsc::Sender<Bytes>,
54	        control_tx: std_mpsc::Sender<PtyIoControlCommand>,
55	        accepting: Arc<Mutex<bool>>,
56	    }
57	
58	    impl PtyIoActorHandle {
59	        pub(crate) async fn write_user_input(
60	            &self,
61	            bytes: Bytes,
62	        ) -> Result<(), mpsc::error::SendError<Bytes>> {
63	            if !*self
64	                .accepting
65	                .lock()
66	                .unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner())
67	            {
68	                return Err(mpsc::error::SendError(bytes));
69	            }
70	            self.data_tx.send(bytes).await
71	        }
72	
73	        pub(crate) fn try_write_user_input(
74	            &self,
75	            bytes: Bytes,
76	        ) -> Result<(), mpsc::error::TrySendError<Bytes>> {
77	            if !*self
78	                .accepting
79	                .lock()
80	                .unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner())
81	            {
82	                return Err(mpsc::error::TrySendError::Closed(bytes));
83	            }
84	            self.data_tx.try_send(bytes)
85	        }
86	
87	        pub(crate) fn resize(
88	            &self,
89	            rows: u16,
90	            cols: u16,
91	            cell_width_px: u32,
92	            cell_height_px: u32,
93	            terminal_responses: Vec<Bytes>,
94	        ) {
95	            let _ = self
96	                .control_tx
97	                .send(PtyIoControlCommand::Resize(PtyResizeRequest {
98	                    resize: PtyResize {
99	                        rows,
100	                        cols,
101	                        cell_width_px,
102	                        cell_height_px,
103	                    },
104	                    terminal_responses,
105	                }));
106	        }
107	
108	        pub(crate) fn shutdown(&self) {
109	            if let Ok(mut accepting) = self.accepting.lock() {
110	                *accepting = false;
111	            }
112	            let _ = self.control_tx.send(PtyIoControlCommand::Shutdown);
113	        }
114	    }
115	
116	    pub(crate) struct PtyIoActor;
117	
118	    impl PtyIoActor {
119	        pub(crate) fn spawn(config: PtyIoActorConfig) -> std::io::Result<PtyIoActorHandle> {
120	            let PtyIoActorConfig {
121	                pane_id,
122	                master,
123	                initially_quiesced,
124	                mut on_read,
125	                on_reader_exit,
126	            } = config;
127	
128	            let mut reader = master
129	                .try_clone_reader()
130	                .map_err(|err| std::io::Error::other(err.to_string()))?;
131	            let writer = master
132	                .take_writer()
133	                .map_err(|err| std::io::Error::other(err.to_string()))?;
134	            let writer = Arc::new(Mutex::new(writer));
135	            let (data_tx, mut data_rx) = mpsc::channel::<Bytes>(1024);
136	            let (control_tx, control_rx) = std_mpsc::channel::<PtyIoControlCommand>();
137	            let accepting = Arc::new(Mutex::new(!initially_quiesced));
138	
139	            {
140	                let writer = Arc::clone(&writer);
141	                std::thread::spawn(move || {
142	                    while let Some(bytes) = data_rx.blocking_recv() {
143	                        if write_all_locked(&writer, &bytes).is_err() {
144	                            break;
145	                        }
146	                    }
147	                    debug!(pane_id, "windows pty writer thread exiting");
148	                });
149	            }
150	
151	            {
152	                let writer = Arc::clone(&writer);
153	                std::thread::spawn(move || {
154	                    let mut buf = [0u8; 8192];
155	                    loop {
156	                        match reader.read(&mut buf) {
157	                            Ok(0) => break,
158	                            Ok(n) => {
159	                                let result = on_read(&buf[..n]);
160	                                for response in result.terminal_responses {
161	                                    if write_all_locked(&writer, &response).is_err() {
162	                                        break;
163	                                    }
164	                                }
165	                            }
166	                            Err(err) => {
167	                                debug!(pane_id, err = %err, "windows pty reader failed");
168	                                break;
169	                            }
170	                        }
171	                    }
172	                    if let Some(on_reader_exit) = on_reader_exit {
173	                        on_reader_exit();
174	                    }
175	                    debug!(pane_id, "windows pty reader thread exiting");
176	                });
177	            }
178	
179	            {
180	                let writer = Arc::clone(&writer);
181	                std::thread::spawn(move || {
182	                    for command in control_rx {
183	                        match command {
184	                            PtyIoControlCommand::Resize(request) => {
185	                                let size = request.resize;
186	                                if let Err(err) = master.resize(PtySize {
187	                                    rows: size.rows,
188	                                    cols: size.cols,
189	                                    pixel_width: size.cell_width_px.min(u16::MAX as u32) as u16,
190	                                    pixel_height: size.cell_height_px.min(u16::MAX as u32) as u16,
191	                                }) {
192	                                    warn!(pane_id, err = %err, "windows pty resize failed");
193	                                }
194	                                for response in request.terminal_responses {
195	                                    if write_all_locked(&writer, &response).is_err() {
196	                                        break;
197	                                    }
198	                                }
199	                            }
200	                            PtyIoControlCommand::Shutdown => break,
201	                        }
202	                    }
203	                    debug!(pane_id, "windows pty control thread exiting");
204	                });
205	            }
206	
207	            Ok(PtyIoActorHandle {
208	                data_tx,
209	                control_tx,
210	                accepting,
211	            })
212	        }
213	    }
214	
215	    fn write_all_locked(
216	        writer: &Arc<Mutex<Box<dyn Write + Send>>>,
217	        bytes: &[u8],
218	    ) -> std::io::Result<()> {
219	        let mut writer = writer
220	            .lock()
221	            .unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner());
222	        writer.write_all(bytes)?;
223	        writer.flush()
224	    }
225	
226	    #[allow(dead_code)]
227	    fn _assert_duration_send(_: Duration) {}
228	}
229	
230	#[cfg(windows)]
231	pub(crate) use windows::*;
232	
203	                    debug!(pane_id, "windows pty control thread exiting");
204	                });
205	            }
206	
207	            Ok(PtyIoActorHandle {
208	                data_tx,
209	                control_tx,
210	                accepting,
211	            })
212	        }
213	    }
214	
215	    fn write_all_locked(
216	        writer: &Arc<Mutex<Box<dyn Write + Send>>>,
217	        bytes: &[u8],
218	    ) -> std::io::Result<()> {
219	        let mut writer = writer
220	            .lock()
221	            .unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner());
222	        writer.write_all(bytes)?;
223	        writer.flush()
224	    }
225	
226	    #[allow(dead_code)]
227	    fn _assert_duration_send(_: Duration) {}
228	}
229	
230	#[cfg(windows)]
231	pub(crate) use windows::*;
232

#044assistant
Now I have a clear picture. Let me lay out what I found before jumping to any build steps, because there's a fundamental blocker. ## What's actually here The `__references` folder holds **fragments of a larger Rust project**, not a buildable project: - `platform-windows.rs` — a Windows platform-abstraction module (clipboard, process enumeration via ToolHelp, process termination, `windows-sys` FFI). It has `use super::{ClipboardImage, ForegroundJob, Signal};` — so it's a submodule of a `platform` module that **isn't here**. - `pty/` — PTY handling: `actor.rs`, `backend.rs`, `backend/unix.rs`, `fd.rs`, `mod.rs`. These use `portable_pty`, `bytes`, `tokio`, `tracing`, and reference `pub(crate)` items — meaning they're internal modules of a crate whose **root and `Cargo.toml` aren't here**. Every other folder (`__solutions`, `__research`, `__files.ccg`, `__files.pi`, `.pi`) is empty scaffolding (just `.gitkeep`). ## The blocker **You can't build this as-is.** There's no `Cargo.toml`, no crate root (`main.rs`/`lib.rs`), and these files depend on sibling modules (`ClipboardImage`, `ForegroundJob`, `Signal`, the `platform` parent, the rest of the crate) that aren't present. These are clearly reference excerpts pulled from an upstream project — "herdr" (likely a terminal multiplexer / pane manager, given the PTY + pane_id + terminal-response code). So "build it first" isn't possible from this folder. I need to know where the **full** project is.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about herdr:

## Overview

Herdr is an agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal. It's a single Rust binary (~10MB) that runs inside the terminal you already use — iTerm, Terminal.app, Alacritty, WezTerm, whatever.

## Key Features

Every agent at a glance — blocked, working, done. You can detach, agents keep running — reattach from any terminal, or over ssh. Sessions survive restarts.

It supports keyboard and mouse, both first-class — tmux-style prefix keys and click, drag, split.

## GitHub Repository

The main GitHub repository is at **https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr**

Herdr accumulated 12.2k stars and 728 forks on GitHub by late June 2026, and went viral on GitHub Trending in late June 2026 (ranked #1 in Rust, #17 overall daily).

## Supported Agents

You can run Claude Code, Codex, Copilot CLI, Cursor Agent, and 15+ AI coding agents in a single terminal with real-time state tracking.

## Installation

Install with curl -fsSL https://herdr.dev/install.sh | sh — it's a single ~10MB binary with zero dependencies.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

## Supported Agents

You can run Claude Code, Codex, Copilot CLI, Cursor Agent, and 15+ AI coding agents in a single terminal with real-time state tracking.

## Installation

Install with curl -fsSL https://herdr.dev/install.sh | sh — it's a single ~10MB binary with zero dependencies.

{
  "url": "https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr",
  "prompt": "What is the build system and toolchain? Report: Rust edition/toolchain version, whether it uses Cargo workspace, build/install instructions, any mention of Windows platform support or Windows build status, and the default branch name. Also note any prerequisites or system dependencies."
}
# Herdr Build System & Toolchain Report

## Build System
The project uses **Cargo** as its build system with a workspace structure. Build instructions are straightforward:

```
git clone https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr
cd herdr
cargo build --release
```

## Rust Toolchain
A `rust-toolchain.toml` file is present in the repository root, though the specific version isn't visible in the provided content. The codebase is "85.6% Rust" by composition.

## Default Branch
The active development branch is **master**.

## Windows Support
Windows platform support exists in beta form. Users can install via: `"powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -c "irm https://herdr.dev/install.ps1 | iex""`. Binary releases are available through multiple channels (Homebrew, mise, GitHub releases).

## Development Prerequisites
The repository includes:
- `Cargo.toml` and `Cargo.lock` for dependency management
- `justfile` for task automation
- Testing via `just test` and code quality checks with `just check`
- `.githooks` for Git workflow automation

## Installation Methods
- Curl script: `curl -fsSL https://herdr.dev/install.sh | sh`
- Homebrew: `brew install herdr`
- Mise: `mise use -g herdr`
- Direct binary downloads from releases page

No explicit system dependencies are documented in the visible content.

name = "herdr"
version = "0.7.4"
edition = "2021"
build = "build.rs"

license = "AGPL-3.0-or-later"

]

=== HEAD ===
0409565 2026-07-17 13:39:27 +0000 docs: update preview manifest

=== rust-toolchain.toml ===
[toolchain]
channel = "1.96.1"
components = ["clippy", "rustfmt"]

=== root Cargo.toml ===
[package]
name = "herdr"
version = "0.7.4"
edition = "2021"
build = "build.rs"
description = "terminal workspace manager for AI coding agents"
license = "AGPL-3.0-or-later"
repository = "https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr"
homepage = "https://herdr.dev"
keywords = ["terminal", "tui", "ai", "agents", "multiplexer"]
categories = ["command-line-utilities"]
include = [
    "src/**/*",
    "assets/sounds/*",
    "docs/next/api/herdr-api.schema.json",
    "README.md",
    "LICENSE",
    "Cargo.toml",
]

[dependencies]
base64 = "0.22.1"
bincode = { version = "2", features = ["serde"] }
bytes = "1"
clap = { version = "4.5", default-features = false, features = ["std"] }
clap_complete = "4.5"
crossterm = "0.29"
ctrlc = "3"
interprocess = "2.4.2"
libc = "0.2"
portable-pty = "=0.9.0"
png = "0.17"
ratatui = { version = "0.30", features = ["unstable-rendered-line-info"] }
regex = "1"
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"
sha2 = "0.10"
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt-multi-thread", "macros", "sync", "time"] }
toml = "0.8"
tracing = "0.1.44"
tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3.23", features = ["env-filter"] }
unicode-width = "0.2"
schemars = { version = "1.2.1", features = ["derive"] }

[patch.crates-io]
portable-pty = { path = "vendor/portable-pty" }

[target.'cfg(windows)'.dependencies]
windows-sys = { version = "0.61.2", features = [
    "Wdk_System_Threading",
    "Win32_Foundation",
    "Win32_System_DataExchange",
    "Win32_System_Diagnostics_Debug",
    "Win32_System_Diagnostics_ToolHelp",
    "Win32_System_Console",
    "Win32_System_JobObjects",
    "Win32_System_Kernel",
    "Win32_System_Memory",
    "Win32_System_Ole",
    "Win32_System_Pipes",
    "Win32_System_Threading",
    "Win32_UI_Shell",
    "Win32_UI_WindowsAndMessaging",
] }

#077assistant
Key facts: - Toolchain pin: **1.96.1** (I have 1.93.0 — `rustup` will auto-install the pinned one on first build). - It's a **single-package crate** (not a multi-crate workspace) with a **vendored `portable-pty`** patch. - The Windows deps (`windows-sys`) are already declared — confirming the `__references` files I deleted were indeed excerpts of what's already upstream. Let me confirm the Windows platform + pty modules already exist in the tree (so we're building the real thing), check `rustup` can fetch the pinned toolchain, then kick off the release build.
#078tool_use
⚙ PowerShell
{
  "command": "cd \"K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\herdr\"\n\"=== windows platform + pty files present? ===\"\nGet-ChildItem -Recurse src -Filter *.rs | Where-Object { $_.Name -match 'windows|pty' } | Select-Object -ExpandProperty FullName | ForEach-Object { $_.Replace(\"K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\herdr\\\",\"\") }\n\"`n=== rustup toolchains ===\"\n$rustup = (Get-Command rustup -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)?.Source\nif ($rustup) { rustup toolchain list } else { \"rustup NOT found - toolchain pin 1.96.1 cannot auto-install\" }",
  "description": "Check windows/pty source and rustup"
}
=== windows platform + pty files present? ===
src\client\input\windows_vti.rs
src\pane\terminal\windows_recent_fallback.rs
src\platform\windows.rs

#080assistant
(no content)
#081assistant
The Windows source files are all present in the upstream tree (`src/platform/windows.rs`, plus Windows input and terminal fallback modules), and `rustup` just auto-installed the pinned **1.96.1** toolchain. The MSVC target `1.96.1-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc` is ready. One thing to confirm before a long release build: the MSVC linker. `windows-sys` + MSVC target needs the MSVC build tools (`link.exe`). Let me verify that's present, otherwise the build will fail at the link step after a long compile.
#082tool_use
⚙ PowerShell
{
  "command": "$link = Get-Command link.exe -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue\nif ($link) { \"link.exe on PATH: $($link.Source)\" } else { \"link.exe NOT on current PATH (may still be found via cargo's MSVC detection)\" }\n\"`n=== VS / Build Tools installations ===\"\n$vswhere = \"${env:ProgramFiles(x86)}\\Microsoft Visual Studio\\Installer\\vswhere.exe\"\nif (Test-Path $vswhere) {\n  & $vswhere -latest -products * -requires Microsoft.VisualStudio.Component.VC.Tools.x86.x64 -property displayName\n  & $vswhere -latest -products * -requires Microsoft.VisualStudio.Component.VC.Tools.x86.x64 -property installationPath\n} else { \"vswhere not found - VS Build Tools likely not installed\" }",
  "description": "Check MSVC linker / VS Build Tools"
}
link.exe NOT on current PATH (may still be found via cargo's MSVC detection)

=== VS / Build Tools installations ===
Visual Studio Community 2026
C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\18\Community
#084assistant
Visual Studio Community 2026 with the VC x64 tools is installed, so the MSVC linker is available (cargo locates it via the VS installation even though it's not on the base PATH). All prerequisites are satisfied: - ✅ git, cargo, rustc - ✅ Pinned toolchain 1.96.1 (MSVC) installed - ✅ Windows source modules present - ✅ VS 2026 + VC tools (linker) Now the release build. This will compile ~200+ dependency crates from scratch, so it'll take several minutes. I'll run it in the background and check on it.

}

or `brew install herdr` · `mise use -g herdr` · windows beta: `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -c "irm https://herdr.dev/install.ps1 | iex"` · [binaries](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/releases)

#100assistant
That's not a build failure — it's my own mistake. I wrote a bad `Tee-Object` path (`K:\Users\...` doesn't exist; it should be `C:\`). The actual `cargo build` never got to report. Let me re-run cleanly, letting the background task's own output file capture everything.

9	  "notes": "Preview build 2026-07-17-813fec141faa\n\nBuilt from `813fec141faa` on `master`.\nBase stable: v0.7.4\nCompare: https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/compare/e907e6a3664608b34351344dfdfbc0431dab8760...813fec141faabe0615b66c4f1738c0af7581309c\n\n### Fixed\n- Prevent remaining subprocess console flashes\n- Detect grok background work from pinned status\n- Detect grok state from terminal signals\n- Follow foreground process group leader cwd\n- Preserve selection when auto-copy is disabled (#1496)\n- Suppress noninteractive process windows\

9	  "notes": "Preview build 2026-07-17-813fec141faa\n\nBuilt from `813fec141faa` on `master`.\nBase stable: v0.7.4\nCompare: https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/compare/e907e6a3664608b34351344dfdfbc0431dab8760...813fec141faabe0615b66c4f1738c0af7581309c\n\n### Fixed\n- Prevent remaining subprocess console flashes\n- Detect grok background work from pinned status\n- Detect grok state from terminal signals\n- Follow foreground process group leader cwd\n- Preserve selection when auto-copy is disabled (#1496)\n- Suppress noninteractive process windows\n\n### Performance\n- Adopt libghostty integration improvements\n\n### Maintenance\n- Support headless console runners\n- Gate Greptile reviews by contributor approval\n- Update vendored libghostty-vt\n- Extract active tab surface rendering",

#112assistant
Useful context: Windows x86_64 is a first-class preview build target upstream (every preview since ~June ships a `herdr-windows-x86_64.exe`), current protocol 16. So a clean Windows build is expected to work. Let me check on the build progress.

#115assistant
Compiling normally — dependencies are building. This is a large dep tree so it'll take a while. I'll wait for the completion notification rather than polling. While it builds: heads-up on `build.rs`. herdr has a build script that may invoke external tooling (git, or the vendored libghostty-vt C build). Let me quickly check what it does, since that's a common Windows build failure point.
#116tool_use
⚙ Read
{
  "file_path": "K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\herdr\\build.rs"
}
1	use std::env;
2	use std::fs;
3	use std::path::PathBuf;
4	use std::process::Command;
5	
6	fn zig_target(target: &str) -> &str {
7	    match target {
8	        "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu" => "x86_64-linux-gnu",
9	        "aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu" => "aarch64-linux-gnu",
10	        "x86_64-unknown-linux-musl" => "x86_64-linux-musl",
11	        "aarch64-unknown-linux-musl" => "aarch64-linux-musl",
12	        "x86_64-apple-darwin" => "x86_64-macos",
13	        "aarch64-apple-darwin" => "aarch64-macos",
14	        "x86_64-pc-windows-msvc" => "x86_64-windows-msvc",
15	        "aarch64-pc-windows-msvc" => "aarch64-windows-msvc",
16	        other => panic!("unsupported target for libghostty-vt build: {other}"),
17	    }
18	}
19	
20	fn env_bool(name: &str) -> Option<bool> {
21	    match env::var(
1	use std::env;
2	use std::fs;
3	use std::path::PathBuf;
4	use std::process::Command;
5	
6	fn zig_target(target: &str) -> &str {
7	    match target {
8	        "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu" => "x86_64-linux-gnu",
9	        "aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu" => "aarch64-linux-gnu",
10	        "x86_64-unknown-linux-musl" => "x86_64-linux-musl",
11	        "aarch64-unknown-linux-musl" => "aarch64-linux-musl",
12	        "x86_64-apple-darwin" => "x86_64-macos",
13	        "aarch64-apple-darwin" => "aarch64-macos",
14	        "x86_64-pc-windows-msvc" => "x86_64-windows-msvc",
15	        "aarch64-pc-windows-msvc" => "aarch64-windows-msvc",
16	        other => panic!("unsupported target for libghostty-vt build: {other}"),
17	    }
18	}
19	
20	fn env_bool(name: &str) -> Option<bool> {
21	    match env::var(name) {
22	        Ok(value) => match value.to_ascii_lowercase().as_str() {
23	            "1" | "true" | "yes" | "on" => Some(true),
24	            "0" | "false" | "no" | "off" => Some(false),
25	            other => panic!("invalid boolean value for {name}: {other}"),
26	        },
27	        Err(env::VarError::NotPresent) => None,
28	        Err(err) => panic!("failed to read {name}: {err}"),
29	    }
30	}
31	
32	fn main() {
33	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=build.rs");
34	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=vendor/libghostty-vt.vendor.json");
35	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=vendor/libghostty-vt/build.zig");
36	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=vendor/libghostty-vt/build.zig.zon");
37	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=vendor/libghostty-vt/include");
38	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=vendor/libghostty-vt/pkg");
39	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=vendor/libghostty-vt/src");
40	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-changed=vendor/libghostty-vt/VERSION");
41	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=LIBGHOSTTY_VT_OPTIMIZE");
42	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=LIBGHOSTTY_VT_SIMD");
43	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=LIBGHOSTTY_VT_ZIG_SYSTEM_DIR");
44	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=HERDR_BUILD_CHANNEL");
45	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=HERDR_BUILD_ID");
46	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=HERDR_BUILD_COMMIT");
47	    println!("cargo:rerun-if-env-changed=ZIG");
48	
49	    let manifest_dir = PathBuf::from(env::var("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR").expect("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR"));
50	    let vendored_dir = manifest_dir.join("vendor/libghostty-vt");
51	    let optimize = env::var("LIBGHOSTTY_VT_OPTIMIZE").unwrap_or_else(|_| "ReleaseFast".into());
52	    let simd = env_bool("LIBGHOSTTY_VT_SIMD").unwrap_or(true);
53	    let target = env::var("TARGET").expect("TARGET");
54	    let zig_target = zig_target(&target);
55	    let version_string = fs::read_to_string(vendored_dir.join("VERSION"))
56	        .expect("failed to read vendored libghostty-vt VERSION")
57	        .trim()
58	        .to_string();
59	
60	    let zig = env::var("ZIG").unwrap_or_else(|_| "zig".into());
61	    let mut command = Command::new(zig);
62	    command
63	        .arg("build")
64	        .arg("-Demit-lib-vt")
65	        .arg(format!("-Doptimize={optimize}"))
66	        .arg(format!("-Dsimd={simd}"))
67	        .arg(format!("-Dtarget={zig_target}"))
68	        .arg(format!("-Dversion-string={version_string}"))
69	        .arg("-Demit-xcframework=false");
70	    if let Ok(system_dir) = env::var("LIBGHOSTTY_VT_ZIG_SYSTEM_DIR") {
71	        command.arg("--system").arg(system_dir);
72	    }
73	
74	    let status = command
75	        .current_dir(&vendored_dir)
76	        .status()
77	        .expect("failed to execute zig build for vendored libghostty-vt");
78	    assert!(
79	        status.success(),
80	        "zig build for vendored libghostty-vt failed: {status}"
81	    );
82	
83	    let lib_dir = vendored_dir.join("zig-out/lib");
84	    println!("cargo:rustc-link-search=native={}", lib_dir.display());
85	    if target.contains("apple-darwin") {
86	        let static_lib = lib_dir.join("libghostty-vt.a");
87	        println!("cargo:rustc-link-arg={}", static_lib.display());
88	    } else if target.contains("windows-msvc") {
89	        println!("cargo:rustc-link-lib=static=ghostty-vt-static");
90	    } else {
91	        println!("cargo:rustc-link-lib=static=ghostty-vt");
92	    }
93	}
94	
tatus = command
75	        .current_dir(&vendored_dir)
76	        .status()
77	        .expect("failed to execute zig build for vendored libghostty-vt");
78	    assert!(
79	        status.success(),
80	        "zig build for vendored libghostty-vt failed: {status}"
81	    );
82	
83	    let lib_dir = vendored_dir.join("zig-out/lib");
84	    println!("cargo:rustc-link-search=native={}", lib_dir.display());
85	    if target.contains("apple-darwin") {
86	        let static_lib = lib_dir.join("libghostty-vt.a");
87	        println!("cargo:rustc-link-arg={}", static_lib.display());
88	    } else if target.contains("windows-msvc") {
89	        println!("cargo:rustc-link-lib=static=ghostty-vt-static");
90	    } else {
91	        println!("cargo:rustc-link-lib=static=ghostty-vt");
92	    }
93	}

#118assistant
This is the critical dependency I was worried about: **`build.rs` invokes `zig build`** to compile the vendored `libghostty-vt` (a C/Zig terminal VT library) as a static lib. **The build requires the Zig compiler on PATH** (or via `ZIG` env var). If Zig isn't installed, the build will fail at the `herdr` crate's build-script step — after all ~250 dependencies finish compiling. Let me check whether Zig is available now, so I can get ahead of it.

Confirmed: `build.rs:77` panicked with `program not found` — **Zig is missing**. Every Rust dependency compiled fine; the only blocker is the vendored `libghostty-vt` needing Zig ≥ 0.15.2 to build. So the real prerequisite list for building herdr on Windows is: Rust 1.96.1 (✅ have it), MSVC linker (✅ VS 2026), and **Zig 0.15.x (❌ missing)**. I need to install Zig. Let me check the cleanest install path — winget is the least intrusive.

#135assistant
winget has `zig.zig` but only at **0.16.0**. The vendored libghostty-vt declares `minimum_zig_version = 0.15.2`. Zig pre-1.0 has frequent breaking language changes, so 0.16.0 may not build a project pinned to 0.15.2 — this is a real risk. Before I decide, there are two decisions here that are yours to make. Let me lay out the situation and the tradeoffs clearly rather than guess. **The one remaining blocker:** herdr's `build.rs` compiles a vendored `libghostty-vt` via **Zig**, which isn't installed. Everything else (Rust 1.96.1, MSVC linker) is ready. Two questions: 1. **Which Zig version.** The vendored lib wants ≥ 0.15.2. winget offers 0.16.0. Zig has breaking changes between minor versions pre-1.0, so 0.16.0 might fail to compile the 0.15.x-era code. The safer bet is the exact 0.15.x Zig from ziglang.org. 2. **How to install it** — winget (system-wide, needs a possibly-elevated install) vs. a local unzip into the project/scratch (no admin, fully contained, set `ZIG` env var for the build).
#136tool_use
⚙ AskUserQuestion
{
  "questions": [
    {
      "question": "herdr's build compiles a vendored libghostty-vt with Zig (not installed). The vendored lib pins minimum_zig_version = 0.15.2; winget only has 0.16.0. Which Zig should I install to avoid version-mismatch build breaks?",
      "header": "Zig version",
      "multiSelect": false,
      "options": [
        {
          "label": "Zig 0.15.x from ziglang.org (Recommended)",
          "description": "Download the latest 0.15.x release matching the pin. Safest for building 0.15.2-era code — avoids pre-1.0 breaking-change risk. I fetch the official Windows zip and verify it."
        },
        {
          "label": "Zig 0.16.0 via winget",
          "description": "Use winget's zig.zig 0.16.0. One command, but newer than the pin — may hit Zig language/build-API breaking changes that fail the libghostty-vt build. I'd fall back to 0.15.x if it breaks."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "question": "How should Zig be installed on this machine?",
      "header": "Install method",
      "multiSelect": false,
      "options": [
        {
          "label": "Local unzip, no admin (Recommended)",
          "description": "Download the Zig zip and extract to a local tools folder (e.g. under the project or scratchpad), then pass it to the build via the ZIG env var. Fully contained, reversible, no system changes, no elevation."
        },
        {
          "label": "System-wide via winget",
          "description": "Install Zig globally with winget so it's on PATH for all projects. May prompt for elevation. Persists on your machine beyond this project."
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
Your questions have been answered: "herdr's build compiles a vendored libghostty-vt with Zig (not installed). The vendored lib pins minimum_zig_version = 0.15.2; winget only has 0.16.0. Which Zig should I install to avoid version-mismatch build breaks?"="Zig 0.15.x from ziglang.org (Recommended)", "How should Zig be installed on this machine?"="System-wide via winget". {
  "command": "$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'\n$url = \"https://ziglang.org/download/0.15.2/zig-x86_64-windows-0.15.2.zip\"\n$expected = \"3a0ed1e8799a2f8ce2a6e6290a9ff22e6906f8227865911fb7ddedc3cc14cb0c\"\n$dl = \"$env:TEMP\\zig-0.15.2.zip\"\n\"Downloading $url ...\"\n$sw = [System.Diagnostics.Stopwatch]::StartNew()\nInvoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -OutFile $dl\n$sw.Stop()\n\"Downloaded in $([int]$sw.Elapsed.TotalSeconds)s, size=$((Get-Item $dl).Length) bytes\"\n$actual = (Get-FileHash $dl -Algorithm SHA256).Hash.ToLower()\n\"Expected: $expected\"\n\"Actual:   $actual\"\nif ($actual -eq $expected) { \"SHA256 OK\" } else { throw \"SHA256 MISMATCH\" }",
  "description": "Download and verify Zig 0.15.2 zip",
  "timeout": 300000
}

{
  "command": "$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'\n$dl = \"$env:TEMP\\zig-0.15.2.zip\"\n$tmpExtract = \"$env:TEMP\\zig-extract\"\n$dest = \"C:\\zig\"\n\nif (Test-Path $tmpExtract) { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force $tmpExtract }\nExpand-Archive -Path $dl -DestinationPath $tmpExtract -Force\n# zip contains a top-level folder zig-x86_64-windows-0.15.2\\\n$inner = Get-ChildItem $tmpExtract -Directory | Select-Object -First 1\n\"Extracted inner folder: $($inner.Name)\"\n\nif (Test-Path $dest) { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force $dest }\nMove-Item -Path $inner.FullName -Destination $dest\n\"Installed to $dest\"\nGet-ChildItem $dest | Select-Object -First 5 Name\n\n# add to USER PATH if not present\n$userPath = [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable(\"Path\",\"User\")\nif ($userPath -notlike \"*$dest*\") {\n    [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(\"Path\", ($userPath.TrimEnd(';') + \";$dest\"), \"User\")\n    \"Added $dest to USER PATH\"\n} else { \"$dest already on USER PATH\" }\n# make available in current session too\n$env:Path = \"$dest;$env:Path\"\n\"`n=== verify ===\"\n& \"$dest\\zig.exe\" version",

{

#158assistant
Here's the command. Run it in your session with the `!` prefix (or a normal PowerShell prompt). The zip is already downloaded and SHA-verified at `$env:TEMP\zig-0.15.2.zip`, so this just extracts it and puts it on PATH: ```powershell $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop' $dl = "$env:TEMP\zig-0.15.2.zip" $tmpExtract = "$env:TEMP\zig-extract" $dest = "C:\zig" if (Test-Path $tmpExtract) { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force $tmpExtract } Expand-Archive -Path $dl -DestinationPath $tmpExtract -Force $inner = Get-ChildItem $tmpExtract -Directory | Select-Object -First 1 if (Test-Path $dest) { Remove-Item -Recurse -Force $dest } Move-Item -Path $inner.FullName -Destination $dest $userPath = [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path","User") if ($userPath -notlike "*$dest*") { [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("Path", ($userPath.TrimEnd(';') + ";$dest"), "User") "Added $dest to USER PATH" } $env:Path = "$dest;$env:Path" zig version ``` Notes: - If the zip isn't there anymore, re-download first: ```powershell Invoke-WebRequest "https://ziglang.org/download/0.15.2/zig-x86_64-windows-0.15.2.zip" -OutFile "$env:TEMP\zig-0.15.2.zip" ``` - `C:\zig` is a clean, short path Zig likes. If writing to `C:\` needs admin on your setup, either run the shell elevated or swap `$dest` to `"$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Programs\zig"`. - The PATH change applies to **new** shells. This session's cargo build won't see it until you restart the shell — or you can just tell me the install path and I'll pass it to cargo via the `ZIG` env var for the build without needing a restart. Once it's installed, tell me and I'll re-run the release build (`cargo build --release` from the `herdr` folder). Zig was the only missing piece — everything else is ready.

#172assistant
Two things are true: the User PATH has **no** zig entry, and I can't read `C:\` from the sandbox. So either the install script's PATH step didn't run, or `zig.exe` sits in a subfolder. The build doesn't actually need Zig on PATH though — `build.rs` honors a `ZIG` env var pointing straight at the executable. That sidesteps both the PATH issue and the sandbox read limitation. I just need the real path to `zig.exe`. Quick check on your side — run this in your `!` shell and paste the output: ```powershell Get-ChildItem C:\zig,C:\Zip -Recurse -Filter zig.exe -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object -Expand FullName ``` The extracted zip normally lays out as either `C:\zig\zig.exe` (if you moved the *inner* folder's contents) or `C:\
#173user
[Request interrupted by user]
#174assistant
(no content)
#175user
Give me the series of commands (powershell) to install zig into c:\Zig, step by step
#176assistant
Here's the step-by-step. Run each block in your `!` shell (or a normal elevated PowerShell if `C:\` needs admin). The zip is already downloaded and SHA-verified at `$env:TEMP\zig-0.15.2.zip`. **1. Re-download if the zip is gone (skip if it's still there):** ```powershell if (-not (Test-Path "$env:TEMP\zig-0.15.2.zip")) { Invoke-WebRequest "https://ziglang.org/download/0.15.2/zig-x86_64-windows-0.15.2.zip" -OutFile "$env:TEMP\zig-0.15.2.zip" } ``` **2. Extract to a temp folder:** ```powershell Remove-Item -Recurse -Force "$env:TEMP\zig-extract" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue Expand-Archive -Path "$env:TEMP\zig-0.15.2.zip" -DestinationPath "$env:TEMP\zig-extract" -Force ``` **3. Move the extracted contents into `C:\Zig` (flattening the inner `zig-x86_64-windows-0.15.2` folder so you get `C:\Zig\zig.exe`, not `C:\Zig\zig-x86_64-windows-0.15.2\zig.exe`):** ```powershell $inner = Get-ChildItem "$env:TEMP\zig-extract" -Directory | Select-Object -First 1 New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "C:\Zig" | Out-Null Move-Item -Path "$($inner.FullName)\*" -Destination "C:\Zig" -Force ``` **4. Add `C:\Zig` to your user PATH:** ```powershell $userPath = [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path","User") if ($userPath -notlike "*C:\Zig*") { [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("Path", ($userPath.TrimEnd(';') + ";C:\Zig"), "User") } ``` **5. Verify (run this so I can see it worked):** ```powershell & "C:\Zig\zig.exe" version Get-ChildItem C:\Zig\zig.exe | Select-Object FullName, Length ``` Step 5 should print `0.15.2` and show `C:\Zig\zig.exe`. Paste that output back. Once it's confirmed, I'll kick off the release build — I'll pass `ZIG=C:\Zig\zig.exe` to cargo directly, so we don't need to wait on a shell restart for PATH to propagate.

#220assistant
Now it's a **different, deeper failure** — the Zig build itself crashed with an internal panic: ``` thread panic: reached unreachable code C:\Zig\lib\std\Build\Step\Run.zig:662: assert(!std.fs.path.isAbsolute(child_cwd_rel)); error: unable to read results of configure phase ... FileNotFound ``` This is a **known Zig 0.15.x bug on Windows**: `std.Build.Step.Run.convertPathArg` asserts a path is relative, but on Windows an absolute path slips through and trips the `unreachable`. It happens inside libghostty-vt's `build.zig` when it runs a build step with a path arg. So the `minimum_zig_version = 0.15.2` pin is necessary-but-not-sufficient — 0.15.2 has this Windows regression. Before I go down a fix path, let me confirm how the project *actually* builds libghostty-vt on Windows in CI — there may be a required Zig version, env var, or flag I'm missing. The `AGENTS.md`/CI is the authority here.

  "description": "Read vendor manifest and list workflows"
}

#225assistant
Yes — everything we're doing is Windows-only. cargo defaults to the host target (`x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`), so `cargo build --release` only builds the Windows binary; the Linux/macOS entries you saw were just upstream's release manifest, not something we're building. No cross-compiling here. Let me find how upstream CI builds the Windows binary — that's where the correct Zig version/flags will be pinned.
#226tool_use
⚙ Bash
{
  "command": "cd \"K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\herdr\"\necho \"=== zig version pins across repo ===\"\ngrep -rniE \"zig[-_ ]?version|mlugg/setup-zig|goto-bus-stop/setup-zig|zig.*0\\.1[0-9]\" .github/ scripts/ justfile 2>/dev/null | head -40",
  "description": "Find Zig version pins in CI"
}

#236thinking
#237assistant
Back on it. Where we left off: Zig 0.15.2 itself crashed with an internal assert (`Run.zig:662: assert(!std.fs.path.isAbsolute(...))`). I have a concrete theory now: that assert fires when Zig can't make a path *relative* — which on Windows happens when two paths are on **different drives**. Our project is on `K:`, but Zig's global cache (where it fetches `build.zig.zon` dependencies) defaults to `C:\Users\...\AppData\Local\zig`. Cross-drive → `path.relative()` returns an absolute path → assert. CI doesn't hit this because setup-zig puts the cache on the same drive as the workspace. Fix: point Zig's global cache at the `K:` drive. Let me test that theory by running the vendored zig build directly.
#238tool_use

  "description": "Test zig build with same-drive global cache",
  "timeout": 600000
}

C:\Users\Tony Baloney\.claude\projects\K--Downloads---Projects-Mine-herdr4Windows\memory\herdr-windows-build.md
+ ---
+ name: herdr-windows-build
+ description: How to build herdr on this Windows machine — required env vars and the cross-drive Zig cache gotcha
+ metadata:
+   type: project
+ ---
+ 
+ herdr (cloned at `K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\herdr`, upstream ogulcancelik/herdr, master) builds on Windows with `cargo build --release`, but ONLY with:
+ 
+ ```powershell
+ $env:ZIG = "C:\Zig\zig.exe"                # Zig 0.15.2, installed 2026-07-17
+ $env:ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR = "K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\.zig-global-cache"
+ ```
+ 
+ **Why:** `build.rs` compiles vendored `libghostty-vt` via `zig build` (min zig 0.15.2). Zig's default global cache is on `C:` while the project is on `K:`; Windows can't relativize cross-drive paths, so Zig panics with `Run.zig:662 assert(!std.fs.path.isAbsolute(...))`. Same-drive cache fixes it.
+ 
+ **How to apply:** Set both env vars in the same shell before any cargo build/test/clippy of herdr. Toolchain is pinned via rust-toolchain.toml to 1.96.1 (rustup auto-installs). Output: `herdr\target\release\herdr.exe` (~17 MB). Verified working 2026-07-17 (v0.7.4).

+ herdr (cloned at `K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\herdr`, upstream ogulcancelik/herdr, master) builds on Windows with a plain `cargo build --release` — all required setup lives in `K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\.cargo\config.toml` (ancestor config, keeps the clone clean):
  
- ```powershell
- $env:ZIG = "C:\Zig\zig.exe"                # Zig 0.15.2, installed 2026-07-17

+ - `[env]` sets `ZIG = C:/Zig/zig.exe` (Zig 0.15.2, installed 2026-07-17) and `ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR = K:/.../.zig-global-cache`
  
  **Why:** `build.rs` compiles vendored `libghostty-vt` via `zig build` (min zig 0.15.2). Zig's default global cache is on `C:` while the project is on `K:`; Windows can't relativize cross-drive paths, so Zig panics with `Run.zig:662 assert(!std.fs.path.isAbsolute(...))`. Same-drive cache fixes it.
  
- **How to apply:** Set both env vars in the same shell before any cargo build/test/clippy of herdr. Toolchain is pinned via rust-toolchain.toml to 1.96.1 (rustup auto-installs). Output: `herdr\target\release\herdr.exe` (~17 MB). Verified working 2026-07-17 (v0.7.4).
+ **How to apply:** Just `cargo build --release` from inside the project tree — do not delete `.cargo/config.toml`. Toolchain pinned via rust-toolchain.toml to 1.96.1 (rustup auto-installs). Output: `__solutions\target\release\herdr.exe` (~17 MB). Verified working 2026-07-17 (v0.7.4).

K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\README.md
+ # HerdrPlus
+ 
+ A Rust-based terminal workspace manager for AI coding agents — a Windows-first extension of [herdr](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr).
+ 
+ Upstream herdr is an agent multiplexer that runs Claude Code, Codex, Copilot CLI, and 15+ other AI coding agents in a single terminal with real-time state tracking. Windows support upstream is beta; HerdrPlus exists to build, harden, and extend herdr on Windows.
+ 
+ ## Status
+ 
+ - Baseline: upstream herdr v0.7.4 (`master`) builds clean on Windows 11 (x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
+ - Binary output: `__solutions/target/release/herdr.exe`
+ 
+ ## Prerequisites
+ 
+ | Tool | Version | Notes |
+ |---|---|---|
+ | Rust | 1.96.1 | Pinned by `rust-toolchain.toml`; rustup auto-installs |
+ | Zig | 0.15.2 | Compiles the vendored `libghostty-vt`; exact version matters |
+ | VS Build Tools | 2022+ | MSVC linker (any Visual Studio with the VC x64 workload) |
+ 
+ ## Build
+ 
+ ```powershell
+ cd __references/herdr
+ cargo build --release
+ ```
+ 
+ That's it. `.cargo/config.toml` at the repo root handles everything else:
+ - Redirects all build output to `__solutions/target/`
+ - Sets `ZIG` and `ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR` (the cache **must** live on the same drive as the repo — Zig panics on cross-drive relative paths on Windows)
+ 
+ ## Layout
+ 
+ ```
+ __references/herdr/   upstream herdr clone (reference base, not committed)
+ __solutions/          all build output (target dir lives here)
+ __research/           notes and investigation artifacts
+ .cargo/config.toml    build configuration (target-dir + Zig env)
+ ```
+ 
+ ## License
+ 
+ Upstream herdr is AGPL-3.0-or-later. Anything in this repo derived from herdr source carries the same license.

K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\CLAUDE.md
+ # HerdrPlus — project instructions
+ 
+ HerdrPlus is a Windows-first extension of upstream [herdr](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr) (Rust agent terminal multiplexer, AGPL-3.0).
+ 
+ ## Layout
+ 
+ - `__references/herdr/` — full upstream clone (v0.7.4 base, `master`). This is the working codebase; it has its own nested `.git` and is NOT committed to this repo.
+ - `__solutions/` — ALL build output goes here. Cargo's target dir is `__solutions/target/`; the release binary is `__solutions/target/release/herdr.exe`.
+ - `__research/` — investigation notes and artifacts.
+ - `.cargo/config.toml` — ancestor cargo config; applies to every cargo command run inside this tree. Do not delete it.
+ 
+ ## Build
+ 
+ ```powershell
+ cd __references/herdr
+ cargo build --release
+ ```
+ 
+ No env setup needed — `.cargo/config.toml` sets `ZIG` (C:/Zig/zig.exe, 0.15.2) and `ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR` and redirects `target-dir`.
+ 
+ ### Hard-won constraints (do not regress)
+ 
+ - `build.rs` compiles vendored `libghostty-vt` via `zig build`; Zig 0.15.2 exactly (min pin, and 0.16 may break the 0.15-era build script).
+ - `ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR` must be on the K: drive (same drive as the repo). Zig on Windows panics (`Run.zig:662 assert(!std.fs.path.isAbsolute(...))`) when it cannot relativize cross-drive paths.
+ - Toolchain pinned to Rust 1.96.1 via `rust-toolchain.toml` (MSVC target).
+ 
+ ## Conventions
+ 
+ - `Changelog.md` is append-only, Keep a Changelog format. Add an entry for every meaningful change; never rewrite past entries.
+ - We only build for Windows (`x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`, the host default). No cross-compilation.
+ - Upstream docs live in `__references/herdr/docs/`; upstream contributor guidance in `__references/herdr/AGENTS.md`.

File created successfully at: K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\CLAUDE.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\Usage.md
+ # Usage
+ 
+ ## Run
+ 
+ ```powershell
+ K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\__solutions\target\release\herdr.exe
+ ```
+ 
+ herdr is a full-screen TUI — run it in Windows Terminal (or any modern terminal), not through a pipe.
+ 
+ Common commands:
+ 
+ ```powershell
+ herdr.exe --version    # print version
+ herdr.exe --help       # CLI options
+ ```
+ 
+ ## What it does
+ 
+ herdr multiplexes AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Copilot CLI, Cursor Agent, and more) in one terminal:
+ 
+ - Every agent at a glance — blocked, working, done
+ - Detach and reattach from any terminal; sessions survive restarts
+ - tmux-style prefix keys plus first-class mouse support (click, drag, split)
+ 
+ See upstream docs for keybindings and configuration: `__references/herdr/docs/` or <https://herdr.dev>.
+ 
+ ## Rebuild after changes
+ 
+ ```powershell
+ cd __references/herdr
+ cargo build --release
+ ```
+ 
+ Output lands in `__solutions/target/release/herdr.exe`. See `CLAUDE.md` for build constraints (Zig 0.15.2, same-drive cache).

File created successfully at: K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\Usage.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

#350tool_use
⚙ Write
K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\Changelog.md
+ # Changelog
+ 
+ All notable changes to HerdrPlus will be documented in this file.
+ 
+ The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/),
+ and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).
+ 
+ This file is append-only: new entries go on top, existing entries are never rewritten.
+ 
+ ## [0.1.0] - 2026-07-17
+ 
+ ### Added
+ 
+ - Cloned upstream [herdr](https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr) v0.7.4 (`master` @ `0409565`) into `__references/herdr/` as the reference codebase.
+ - Working Windows release build (`x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`, Rust 1.96.1, Zig 0.15.2, VS 2026 MSVC linker) producing `__solutions/target/release/herdr.exe` (~17 MB, verified `herdr 0.7.4`).
+ - `.cargo/config.toml` build configuration: redirects `target-dir` to `__solutions/target/` and sets `ZIG` / `ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR` so a plain `cargo build --release` works with no shell setup.
+ - Project documentation: `README.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `Usage.md`, this changelog.
+ 
+ ### Fixed
+ 
+ - Zig build panic on Windows (`Run.zig:662 assert(!std.fs.path.isAbsolute(...))`) when compiling vendored `libghostty-vt`: Zig's default global cache on `C:` cannot be relativized against the repo on `K:`. Resolved by pinning `ZIG_GLOBAL_CACHE_DIR` to the repo drive.

File created successfully at: K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\Changelog.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

⚙ Write
K:\Downloads\__Projects.Mine\herdr4Windows\.gitignore
+ # Upstream herdr clone — reference base with its own .git, never committed here
+ __references/herdr/
+ 
+ # Build output
+ __solutions/target/
+ target/
+ 
+ # Zig caches
+ .zig-global-cache/
+ .zig-cache/
+ zig-out/
+ 
+ # Rust
+ **/*.rs.bk
+ *.pdb
+ 
+ # Editor / IDE
+ .vscode/
+ .idea/

#370thinking
#371assistant
Done. **HerdrPlus is live: https://github.com/KennethJefferson/HerdrPlus** What was set up: - **Docs**: `README.md` (project overview, prerequisites, build), `CLAUDE.md` (project instructions — layout, build constraints, append-only changelog rule), `Usage.md` (run/rebuild), `Changelog.md` (Keep a Changelog format, first entry `[0.1.0] - 2026-07-17` covering the upstream clone, the working Windows build, and the Zig cross-drive fix). One deviation: I used the standard spelling `Usage.md` instead of "Useage.md" — easy to rename if you meant it literally. - **Git**: repo at the project root, branch `main`, initial commit `init commit` with a descriptive body. - **GitHub**: public repo `HerdrPlus`, description "Rust-based terminal workspace manager for AI coding agents - a Windows-first extension of herdr", pushed. - **`.gitignore`**: excludes the upstream clone (`__references/herdr/` — it's reference material with its own `.git`, and AGPL upstream), all build output (`__solutions/target/`), Zig caches, plus standard Rust/editor/Windows entries. The `.cargo/config.toml` build config *is* committed, since the build depends on it. Also verified earlier: the rebuild from the new `__references\herdr` location completed clean, so the whole pipeline works in the final layout.

The clone currently has its own nested `.git`, which is why git treats it as foreign. To include the actual source in HerdrPlus I'll vendor it: remove the nested `.git` (the upstream commit `0409565` is already recorded in the changelog, so we can always re-clone/diff upstream), drop the ignore rule, and push. [main 3a0bf10] vendor upstream herdr source into __references/herdr

#417thinking
#418assistant
Done — the herdr reference source is now in the GitHub repo: commit `3a0bf10` pushed to https://github.com/KennethJefferson/HerdrPlus (1,860 files, ~685k lines). How it was included: - **Vendored, not submoduled**: removed the nested `.git` from `__references\herdr` so the actual source lives in HerdrPlus (a submodule would only have stored a pointer to upstream). Upstream provenance (`master` @ `0409565...`, v0.7.4 base) is recorded in `CLAUDE.md` and the changelog, so we can always diff against a fresh upstream clone later. - **Still excluded**: generated artifacts (`vendor/libghostty-vt/zig-out`, `.zig-cache`, `__solutions/target/`) — the build regenerates those. - **Docs updated**: `.gitignore` rule dropped, `README.md`/`CLAUDE.md` now say the source is committed, and `Changelog.md` got an appended `[0.1.1]` entry describing the change. One trade-off worth knowing: without the nested `.git`, pulling future upstream updates means cloning upstream separately and diffing/merging manually — which is arguably what you want anyway once HerdrPlus starts diverging with its own features.

#429thinking
#430assistant
herdr is up, maximized in Windows Terminal. Two `herdr` processes are running (PIDs 42304 and 37184) — that's expected, not a duplicate: herdr uses a client/server split, so one is the session server (which survives detach) and the other is the TUI client attached to it. You should be seeing the herdr workspace on screen now. What's the next test — spawn an agent pane (e.g., Claude Code), test splits/mouse, or detach/reattach?

Right — the server keeps sessions alive after the TUI closes. Let me check if herdr has a graceful shutdown command before force-killing:
#442tool_use
⚙ PowerShell
{
  "command": "$exe = \"K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\__solutions\\target\\release\\herdr.exe\"\n& $exe --help 2>&1 | Select-String -Pattern \"kill|shutdown|quit|stop|server|detach\" | ForEach-Object { $_.Line.Trim() }",

herdr server stop
herdr server reload-config
herdr status [server|client]     Show local client and running server status
herdr server stop                Stop the running server via the API socket
herdr server reload-config       Reload config.toml in the running server
herdr server                     Run as headless server
--no-session        Run monolithically (no server/client, escape hatch)
--remote <target>   Attach through SSH to a remote Herdr server
--remote-keybindings <local|server>

#444thinking
#445assistant
`herdr server stop` is the graceful path. Stopping, then verifying nothing lingers:
#446tool_use
⚙ PowerShell
{
  "command": "$exe = \"K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\__solutions\\target\\release\\herdr.exe\"\n& $exe server stop 2>&1\nStart-Sleep -Seconds 2\n$p = Get-Process herdr -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue\nif ($p) { \"still running:\"; $p | Select-Object Id,ProcessName; $p | Stop-Process -Force -Confirm:$false; Start-Sleep -Seconds 1; \"after force-kill: \" + ((Get-Process herdr -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) -eq $null ? \"clean\" : \"STILL ALIVE\") } else { \"all herdr processes exited cleanly\" }",
  "description": "Gracefully stop herdr server, verify cleanup"
}
all herdr processes exited cleanly
#448assistant
Clean shutdown. `herdr server stop` terminated the server via its API socket, and both processes (client + server) exited on their own — no force-kill needed, nothing left in memory. For future reference, the useful lifecycle commands we just confirmed: - `herdr server stop` — graceful full shutdown - `herdr status` — check what's running (client/server) - Logs live at `C:\Users\Tony Baloney\AppData\Roaming\herdr\` (`herdr.log`, `herdr-client.log`, `herdr-server.log`) — handy for e2e debugging. What's the next e2e scenario?

#457thinking
#458assistant
Launched directly this time — herdr owns its own console window (PID 25992 holds the window, titled with the exe path; PID 23752 is the server), started maximized. Since herdr is the process that owns this window, closing herdr should take the window down with it, unlike the WT wrapper. One caveat: Windows 11 sometimes routes console apps into Windows Terminal anyway if WT is set as the system's default terminal. If you're seeing it inside WT again, that's a Settings toggle (Settings → Privacy & security → For developers → Terminal, or WT's own "Default terminal application" setting) rather than something I can override from the launch side. How does it look, and what's next?

herdr — terminal workspace manager for AI coding agents

Usage: herdr [options]
       herdr --session <name> [options]
       herdr --remote <ssh-target> [--session <name>]
       herdr session attach <name>
       herdr completion zsh
       herdr update [--handoff]
       herdr channel set <stable|preview>
       herdr server stop
       herdr server reload-config
       herdr api <subcommand> ...
       herdr completion <shell>
       herdr config <subcommand> ...
       herdr channel <subcommand> ...
       herdr workspace <subcommand> ...
       herdr worktree <subcommand> ...
       herdr tab <subcommand> ...
       herdr notification <subcommand> ...
       herdr agent <subcommand> ...
       herdr pane <subcommand> ...
       herdr wait <subcommand> ...
       herdr session <subcommand> ...
       herdr integration <subcommand> ...

Common commands:
  herdr                            Launch or attach to the persistent session
  herdr status [server|client]     Show local client and running server status
  herdr update                     Download and install the latest version
  herdr completion zsh             Generate shell completions for zsh
  herdr server stop                Stop the running server via the API socket
  herdr channel set <stable|preview> Choose the stable or preview update channel
  herdr server reload-config       Reload config.toml in the running server
  herdr config reset-keys          Back up config.toml and remove custom keybindings
  herdr channel <subcommand>       Manage the stable or preview update channel
  herdr api <subcommand>           Inspect socket API metadata and live runtime state
  herdr workspace <subcommand>     Workspace helpers over the socket API
  herdr worktree <subcommand>      Git worktree helpers over the socket API
  herdr tab <subcommand>           Tab helpers over the socket API
  herdr notification <subcommand>  Notification helpers over the socket API
  herdr agent <subcommand>         Agent/terminal helpers over the socket API
  herdr pane <subcommand>          Pane control helpers over the socket API
  herdr wait <subcommand>          Blocking wait helpers over the socket API
  herdr session <subcommand>       Manage named persistent sessions
  herdr integration <subcommand>   Manage built-in agent integrations

Advanced commands:
  herdr server                     Run as headless server

Options:
  --no-session        Run monolithically (no server/client, escape hatch)
  --session <name>    Use or create a named persistent session
  --remote <target>   Attach through SSH to a remote Herdr server
  --remote-keybindings <local|server>
                      Keybindings for --remote app attach (default: local)
  --handoff           Opt into live handoff for update or remote attach
  --default-config    Print default configuration and exit
  --version, -V       Print version and exit
  --help, -h          Show this help

#499thinking
#500assistant
Done — yes, panes can be created and aligned evenly via the CLI. You should be seeing a 2×2 grid now, verified numerically from the layout API: | Pane | Position | Size | |---|---|---| | `w1:p1` (focused) | top-left | 106×31 | | `w1:p3` | top-right | 105×31 | | `w1:p4` | bottom-left | 106×31 | | `w1:p5` | bottom-right | 105×31 | All three splits sit at exactly ratio 0.5. The 106 vs 105 width is unavoidable — the tab area is 211 columns wide, which is odd, so one column gets the extra cell. Heights are perfectly even. Notable findings from this test: - herdr exposes a full JSON socket API through the CLI (`pane split/list/layout/resize/close`, `agent start/send/read`, `pane send-keys`, `pane run`) — we can drive every future e2e scenario headlessly, no keyboard automation needed. - There's no tmux-style "rebalance all" command; even alignment is achieved by choosing split ratios. If layouts drift, `pane resize` adjusts them after the fact. - The earlier session state persisted (there was already a second tab from our previous runs) — sessions really do survive full server shutdowns; state is on disk, not just in memory. Want me to test something else in this grid — e.g., run a command in each pane, swap/zoom panes, or close them back down to one?

#507tool_use
⚙ PowerShell
{
  "command": "$exe = \"K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\__solutions\\target\\release\\herdr.exe\"\n$panes = ((& $exe pane list | ConvertFrom-Json).result.panes).pane_id\n\"panes: $($panes -join ', ')\"\n$results = $panes | ForEach-Object -Parallel {\n    $out = & $using:exe pane run $_ cls 2>&1\n    \"$_ -> $out\"\n} -ThrottleLimit 8\n$results",
  "description": "Send cls to all panes in parallel"
}
panes: w1:p1, w1:p4, w1:p3, w1:p5
w1:p4 -> 
w1:p1 -> 
w1:p3 ->

#522assistant
Confirmed — all four panes were sent `cls` in parallel and every screen is now clear: each pane's visible content is down to a single fresh PowerShell prompt. How it works: `herdr pane run <id> cls` (which types the command and presses Enter) fired concurrently across panes via `ForEach-Object -Parallel`, and `pane read --source visible` verified the result. One gotcha learned for future scripting: `pane list`/`pane split`/`pane layout` return JSON, but `pane read` returns raw text. Next test?

{

#531assistant
All done in one pass: - Closed `w1:p3`, `w1:p4`, `w1:p5` — each returned `ok`, leaving only the original `w1:p1` (back to a single full-size pane) - `herdr server stop` shut everything down gracefully — no herdr processes left, no force-kill needed E2E so far has covered: launch (direct, maximized), session persistence across restarts, even pane splitting with numeric verification, parallel command dispatch to all panes, pane teardown, and clean shutdown — all driveable headlessly through the socket API. Ready for the next scenario.

2359-fn should_trim_trailing_url_cell(cells: &[TextCell], start: usize, end: usize) -> bool {
2360-    match cells[end].ch {
2361-        '"' | '\'' | '`' | '.' | ',' | ';' | ':' | '!' | '?' => true,
2362:        ')' => !trailing_url_closer_is_balanced(cells, start, end, '(', ')'),
2363:        ']' => !trailing_url_closer_is_balanced(cells, start, end, '[', ']'),
2364:        '}' => !trailing_url_closer_is_balanced(cells, start, end, '{', '}'),
2365-        _ => false,
2366-    }
2367-}
2368-
2369:fn trailing_url_closer_is_balanced(
2370-    cells: &[TextCell],
2371-    start: usize,
2372-    end: usize,
2373-    open: char,
2374-    close: char,
2375-) -> bool {
2376:    let mut balance = 0i32;
2377-    for cell in &cells[start..end] {
2378-        if cell.ch == open {
2379:            balance += 1;
2380-        } else if cell.ch == close {
2381:            balance -= 1;
2382-        }
2383-    }
2384:    balance > 0
2385-}
2386-
2387-fn quoted_path_span_at_column(cells: &[TextCell], clicked_idx: usize) -> Option<CellSpan> {
#553thinking
#554assistant
Manual — there's no tile/balance command in herdr. I verified across all three layers: - **CLI**: `pane` and `tab` subcommands have split/resize/swap/zoom/move, but nothing layout-wide - **API schema** (`herdr-api.schema.json`): zero hits for balance/tile/equalize - **Source** (`src/app/actions.rs` etc.): the only "balance" match is URL bracket-matching code, unrelated to layout So unlike tmux's `select-layout tiled` (what psmux gives you), herdr only exposes primitives. The even 2×2 grid worked because I chose the split *order and ratios* deliberately: split the root at 0.5, then split each resulting column at 0.5. Ratios are relative to the pane being split, so evenness comes from the composition, not from any rebalancing. A naive sequence (split, split, split on the same pane) would give you 1/2, 1/4, 1/4-style cascades. That makes this a natural **first HerdrPlus feature**: a `herdr pane balance` (or `tab tile`) command that walks the split tree and resets ratios so all leaves get equal area — the layout data structure clearly supports it since `pane layout` already exposes the full split tree with mutable ratios (`pane resize` proves they're adjustable at runtime). Want that to be feature #1?

#558user
Is the 0.5 the small margin between each pane so that user can easily identify each pace?
#559assistant
(no content)
#560assistant
(no content)
#561thinking
#562assistant
No — the 0.5 is not a margin. It's the **split proportion**: when you split a pane, `--ratio 0.5` means "divide this pane's space 50/50 between the two resulting panes." A ratio of 0.3 would give one pane 30% and the other 70%. That's why using 0.5 at every split produced the even grid. The visible separation between panes is a different mechanism. Looking at the layout numbers from our test: the left column spanned x=26–131 (width 106) and the right column started at exactly x=132 — the pane rectangles are directly adjacent with **zero gap**. The lines you see between panes are 1-character border cells that the TUI draws *inside* each pane's rectangle (standard ratatui approach), plus the focused pane gets a highlighted border so you can identify it. So identification comes from drawn borders and focus highlighting, not from spacing. In cell-based terminal UIs there's no such thing as a sub-character margin anyway — everything is a grid of character cells, so a "margin" would cost a full blank row/column. herdr spends that cell on a border line instead, which is the norm for terminal multiplexers.

{
  "command": "$exe = \"K:\\Downloads\\__Projects.Mine\\herdr4Windows\\__solutions\\target\\release\\herdr.exe\"\nStart-Process $exe -WindowStyle Maximized\nStart-Sleep -Seconds 4\n& $exe pane split w1:p1 --direction right --ratio 0.5 | Out-Null\n& $exe pane split w1:p1 --direction down --ratio 0.5 | Out-Null\n$newRight = ((& $exe pane list | ConvertFrom-Json).result.panes | Where-Object { $_.pane_id -notin \"w1:p1\" } | Sort-Object pane_id | Select-Object -First 1).pane_id\n# split the right column down; find it via layout instead to be safe\n$layout = (& $exe pane layout --pane w1:p1 | ConvertFrom-Json).result.layout\n$rightTop = ($layout.panes | Where-Object { $_.rect.x -gt 26 } | Sort-Object { $_.rect.y } | Select-Object -First 1).pane_id\n& $exe pane split $rightTop --direction down --ratio 0.5 | Out-Null\n\"--- final grid ---\"\n(& $exe pane layout --pane w1:p1 | ConvertFrom-Json).result.layout.panes | ForEach-Object { \"{0}  x={1} y={2}  {3}x{4}\" -f $_.pane_id, $_.rect.x, $_.rect.y, $_.rect.width, $_.rect.height }",
  "description": "Relaunch herdr maximized, rebuild even 2x2 grid"
}

That matches the layout data exactly — the screenshot confirms everything working: - **2×2 grid, even**: four PowerShell panes with the drawn 1-cell borders between them (you can see there's no actual gap — just the border lines, as discussed) - **Focus indication**: top-left pane (`w1:p1`) has the cursor and its border reads as the active one - **Left sidebar**: herdr's workspace panel showing the `herdr4Windows` workspace on `main`, the `spaces`/`agents` sections, and the tab strip with tab `1` — this is the agent-state-at-a-glance UI that upstream herdr is built around - **Title bar**: herdr owns its own window (title is the exe path), so no Windows Terminal wrapper this time Also visible: the sidebar takes ~26 columns, which is exactly why the pane area started at x=26 in the layout JSON. Want me to tear it down (close panes + `server stop`), or keep it up and try another scenario — e.g., launching an actual agent (`herdr agent start`) in one of the panes?
